title: Recognizing National Foster Care Month as an opportunity to raise awareness about the challenges of children in the foster care system and encouraging Congress to implement policy to improve the lives of children in, or at risk of entering, the foster care system.
plain_summary: This bill recognizes National Foster Care Month as a time to learn about the problems children in foster care face. It talks about how many children are in the foster care system and the challenges they deal with. The bill asks Congress to make better rules and policies to help these children. It also wants Congress to keep supporting programs that help families and help children in foster care find safe, permanent homes.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution recognizes National Foster Care Month as a chance to raise awareness about the challenges children face in the foster care system. It encourages Congress to create policies that improve the lives of children who are in foster care or might enter foster care.

sections.title: Background Information
sections.text: National Foster Care Month was created more than thirty years ago to bring attention to foster care issues, highlight how important it is for every child to have a permanent family, and recognize the important work of foster parents, social workers, and advocates. All children deserve a safe, loving, and permanent family. The main goal of the foster care system is to keep children safe while working to find them a safe, loving, and permanent home. In two thousand twenty two, about three hundred sixty nine thousand children were officially living in foster care. This number does not count children who were placed with caregivers outside the official foster care system. About one hundred ninety seven thousand children entered foster care in two thousand twenty two, while one hundred nine thousand children were eligible and waiting for adoption at the end of that year. Children of color are more likely to stay in the foster care system longer and are less likely to be reunited with their birth families. Foster parents, including family members who take in children, are the main caregivers for children who cannot safely stay with their birth parents. They provide physical care, emotional support, help with education, and are the biggest source of permanent homes for children leaving foster care through adoption. Children in foster care who are placed with relatives have more stability than those placed with non relatives. They have fewer placement changes, feel better about where they live, are more likely to stay with their brothers and sisters, and have fewer behavior problems. However, some relative caregivers receive less money and support services than regular foster caregivers. Recent studies show that foster children on Medicaid were given antipsychotic medications at nearly four times the rate of other children on Medicaid. Youth in foster care face educational problems, with sixty five percent of former foster children experiencing at least seven school changes while in care. There needs to be more focus on prevention and reunification services to reduce the number of children entering or reentering the foster care system. The COVID nineteen pandemic created additional challenges for youth and families in the child welfare system, including delays in finding permanent homes, money problems, and disruptions in education. More than twenty thousand youth age out of foster care each year without a legal permanent connection to an adult or family, and twenty percent of them become homeless immediately. The number of youth aging out of foster care has steadily increased for the past ten years. Foster care is meant to be temporary, but children stay in the system for an average of nineteen point six months. Children in foster care experience an average of three different placements, which often disrupts their routines and requires them to change schools and move away from siblings, extended family, and familiar places. Children entering foster care often face the wrong belief that they are disruptive, unruly, and dangerous, even though they are placed in foster care because of what a parent or guardian did, not because of anything the child did. Children who age out of foster care do not have the security or support of a birth or adoptive family and often struggle to find affordable housing, get health insurance, pursue higher education, and find good jobs. On average, eight point five percent of positions in child protection services remain empty. Because of heavy caseloads and limited resources, the average time a worker stays in child protection services is just three years. States, local areas, and communities should be encouraged to invest resources in prevention and reunification services and programs that help after children find permanent homes to ensure more children in foster care get safe, loving, and permanent placements. In two thousand eighteen, Congress passed the Family First Prevention Services Act, which provided new money for prevention, early intervention, and family reunification services to help more families stay together. It also provided support for kinship caregivers (relatives who care for children) and better and more flexible services for older youth aging out of foster care to ensure more children can be in safe, loving, and permanent homes. Federal laws over the past three decades have provided new investments and services to improve outcomes for children in the foster care system. May is a good month to designate as National Foster Care Month to provide a chance to acknowledge the accomplishments of child welfare workers, foster parents, advocates, and mentors for their dedication and positive effects on children's lives. Much still needs to be done to ensure all children have a safe, loving, nurturing, and permanent family, regardless of age or special needs.

sections.title: House Resolution
sections.text: The House of Representatives supports designating National Foster Care Month. It recognizes National Foster Care Month as a chance to raise awareness about the challenges children face in the foster care system. It encourages Congress to create policies to improve the lives of children in the foster care system. It acknowledges the special needs of children in the foster care system. It recognizes foster youth throughout the United States for their ongoing strength, courage, and resilience while facing life challenges. It acknowledges the exceptional former foster children who serve as advocates and role models for youth who remain in foster care. It honors the commitment and dedication of individuals who work tirelessly to provide help and services to children in the foster care system. It reaffirms the need to continue working to improve outcomes for all children in the foster care system through parts B and E of title IV of the Social Security Act and other programs designed to support vulnerable families, invest in prevention and early intervention and reunification services, promote adoption when reunification is not in the child's best interests, adequately serve children brought into the foster care system, and help children who age out of the foster care system successfully transition into adulthood.
